questions.question: What was the Jerusalem factor?

choices.text: The resurrection message began in the very city where Jesus had been publicly executed and buried
choices.feedback: Correct. The claim was proclaimed where it could be checked most directly.

choices.text: Jerusalem was the only city in the Roman Empire where crucifixions took place
choices.feedback: Not quite. Crucifixion was practiced throughout the Roman world.

choices.text: The disciples avoided Jerusalem because no one there could check their claims
choices.feedback: Not quite. The point is almost the opposite: Jerusalem was the place where the claim could be checked.

choices.text: The resurrection was first preached centuries later in a distant city
choices.feedback: Not quite. The resurrection proclamation was early and centered in Jerusalem.

questions.explanation: The Jerusalem factor means the resurrection was proclaimed in the same city where Jesus had been killed and buried. If the tomb still contained Jesus' body, the claim could have been challenged immediately.

questions.question: What is the principle of embarrassment?

choices.text: A report is more likely to be authentic when it includes details awkward or damaging to the author's side
choices.feedback: Correct. People are less likely to invent details that embarrass their own side.

choices.text: A report is more reliable when it removes embarrassing details
choices.feedback: Not quite. Removing embarrassing details may make a story smoother, but it does not increase credibility.

choices.text: A report is historical only if it makes religious leaders look good
choices.feedback: Not quite. Embarrassing or inconvenient details can sometimes support authenticity.

choices.text: A report should be rejected whenever it contains uncomfortable information
choices.feedback: Not quite. Uncomfortable details may actually count in favor of authenticity.

questions.explanation: The principle of embarrassment says people are less likely to invent details that weaken their own case, embarrass themselves, or make their side look bad.

questions.question: When can an event reasonably be considered historical?

choices.text: When it is supported by strong evidence
choices.feedback: True, but incomplete. Strong evidence matters, but historical reasoning also weighs context and rival explanations.

choices.text: When it fits the known historical context
choices.feedback: True, but incomplete. Historical fit matters, but it is not the whole test by itself.

choices.text: When it is better explained by that event than by rival explanations
choices.feedback: True, but incomplete. Explanatory power matters, but it works together with evidence and context.

choices.text: A, B, and C
choices.feedback: Correct. A historical conclusion is strongest when evidence, context, and explanatory power all work together.

questions.explanation: A historical conclusion is strongest when it is supported by evidence, fits the context, and explains the known data better than competing theories.

questions.question: What is the minimal facts approach?

choices.text: An argument that uses only facts supported by strong evidence and widely granted by relevant scholars
choices.feedback: Correct. The approach builds from facts that have strong evidence and broad scholarly acceptance.

choices.text: An argument that assumes the Bible is inspired before examining the evidence
choices.feedback: Not quite. The minimal facts approach does not require the skeptic to first accept biblical inspiration.

choices.text: An argument that avoids history and relies only on personal experience
choices.feedback: Not quite. The approach is historical and evidential.

choices.text: An argument that says every Gospel detail must first be proven beyond doubt
choices.feedback: Not quite. The approach does not require proving every Gospel detail before considering the resurrection.

questions.explanation: The minimal facts approach builds a case from facts that have strong evidential support and are widely accepted by scholars, including many who are not Christians.

questions.question: If a skeptic says, "I don't believe in the resurrection because I don't believe in the Bible," what could a Christian say?

choices.text: You do not have to assume the Bible is inspired to examine its documents as historical sources
choices.feedback: Correct. The documents can be examined historically before someone accepts them as Scripture.

choices.text: Then there is no point discussing historical evidence
choices.feedback: Not quite. The minimal facts approach is designed to keep the historical discussion open.

choices.text: The resurrection can only be discussed after someone already accepts Christianity
choices.feedback: Not quite. The resurrection can be investigated historically before someone accepts Christianity.

choices.text: The best response is to avoid evidence and appeal only to feelings
choices.feedback: Not quite. Personal experience may matter, but this approach emphasizes public historical evidence.

questions.explanation: A Christian can say that the New Testament documents may be examined historically, even by someone who does not yet believe they are inspired Scripture.

questions.question: True or false: If Jesus did not rise from the dead, then Christianity is false.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Christianity stands or falls on the resurrection.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Not quite. The New Testament treats the resurrection as essential, not optional.

questions.explanation: Christianity stands or falls on the resurrection. Paul says that if Christ has not been raised, Christian faith is futile.

questions.question: What is the role of evidence in investigating the truth of Christianity?

choices.text: Evidence helps test whether Christianity's central historical claims are true
choices.feedback: Correct. Christianity makes historical claims that can be investigated.

choices.text: Evidence is irrelevant because Christianity has no historical claims
choices.feedback: Not quite. Christianity is deeply connected to historical claims.

choices.text: Evidence should only be used after someone already agrees with the conclusion
choices.feedback: Not quite. Good investigation does not require agreement before looking at evidence.

choices.text: Evidence can prove every Christian doctrine with mathematical certainty
choices.feedback: Not quite. Historical evidence usually gives reasonable confidence, not mathematical certainty.

questions.explanation: Christianity makes historical claims, especially about Jesus' death and resurrection. Evidence helps us investigate whether those claims are true.

questions.question: Why is the minimal facts approach effective?

choices.text: It argues from facts that are strongly evidenced and often accepted even by critical scholars
choices.feedback: Correct. It begins with common ground rather than assuming the listener already agrees.

choices.text: It depends on first proving biblical inerrancy
choices.feedback: Not quite. The approach does not begin by requiring belief in inerrancy.

choices.text: It avoids the need to discuss actual historical data
choices.feedback: Not quite. It depends directly on historical data.

choices.text: It works only if the listener already agrees that miracles happen
choices.feedback: Not quite. It can be used with skeptics because it begins with evidence rather than assuming agreement about miracles.

questions.explanation: The approach is effective because it starts with common ground: facts that have strong historical support and are often accepted even by scholars who do not share Christian beliefs.

questions.question: What is the principle of enemy attestation?

choices.text: A claim gains credibility when it is acknowledged by a hostile or unsympathetic source
choices.feedback: Correct. A hostile source is unlikely to invent a claim that helps the side it opposes.

choices.text: A claim is false whenever an enemy reports it
choices.feedback: Not quite. A hostile source can sometimes strengthen a claim.

choices.text: A claim is true only when it is reported by a friend
choices.feedback: Not quite. Friendly sources can matter, but enemy attestation has its own value.

choices.text: A claim should be ignored unless all enemies agree about it
choices.feedback: Not quite. Historical arguments rarely require every hostile source to agree.

questions.explanation: Enemy attestation matters because hostile sources are unlikely to invent claims that help the side they oppose.

questions.question: What is the principle of multiple attestation?

choices.text: A claim is stronger when it is supported by multiple independent sources or lines of evidence
choices.feedback: Correct. Multiple independent sources or lines of evidence can strengthen a historical claim.

choices.text: A claim is weaker when more than one source mentions it
choices.feedback: Not quite. Multiple independent sources usually strengthen, not weaken, a historical claim.

choices.text: A claim is historical only if every source uses identical wording
choices.feedback: Not quite. Identical wording may suggest copying; independent testimony can be more valuable.

choices.text: A claim should be accepted only if it appears in one source
choices.feedback: Not quite. A single source may matter, but multiple independent sources generally provide stronger support.

questions.explanation: Multiple attestation means a claim gains strength when it appears in more than one independent source or stream of evidence.

lessons.title: Early Testimony and the Disciples’ Conviction

questions.question: How soon after Jesus' death did Paul likely receive the creedal tradition quoted in 1 Corinthians 15:3-5?

choices.text: Within about five years
choices.feedback: Correct. Many scholars date this tradition very early, often within just a few years of Jesus' death.

choices.text: Within about fifty years
choices.feedback: Not quite. That is much later than the early dating often argued for this creed.

choices.text: Sometime in the second century
choices.feedback: Not quite. The creed is usually dated far earlier than the second century.

choices.text: During the Middle Ages
choices.feedback: Not quite. That would be wildly too late for Paul's own letter.

questions.explanation: The tradition Paul quotes in 1 Corinthians 15:3-5 is widely regarded as very early. Habermas and Licona argue that Paul probably received it within about five years of Jesus' death, making it important evidence against the idea that resurrection belief developed only much later.

questions.question: According to Habermas and Licona, how many sources attest to the original disciples' willingness to suffer and/or die for their beliefs?

choices.text: Seven
choices.feedback: Correct. The argument appeals to seven sources for the disciples' willingness to suffer and/or die.

choices.text: Two
choices.feedback: Not quite. The case appeals to more than two sources.

choices.text: Four
choices.feedback: Not quite. The number given in the argument is higher.

choices.text: Twenty-seven
choices.feedback: Not quite. That would overstate the number in this specific argument.

questions.explanation: Habermas and Licona argue that seven sources attest to the original disciples' willingness to suffer and/or die for their beliefs. The point is not that every detail of every apostle's death is known, but that the disciples' public commitment under pressure is historically significant.

questions.question: True or false: The disciples' willingness to suffer or die for their beliefs proves that their beliefs were true.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Not quite. Sincerity does not by itself prove that a belief is true.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Correct. Their willingness shows sincerity, not automatic truth.

questions.explanation: People can sincerely die for false beliefs. The important point is that the disciples' willingness to suffer supports the claim that they truly believed they had encountered the risen Jesus. It argues against deliberate fraud, but it does not by itself prove the resurrection.

questions.question: What can we say with historical confidence about the original disciples' willingness to suffer for their resurrection claim?

choices.text: They preached the gospel despite threats, opposition, and violence recorded in early sources
choices.feedback: Correct. The key point is their continued proclamation under real pressure.

choices.text: We know exactly how every original disciple died
choices.feedback: Not quite. The details of every disciple's death are not equally well known.

choices.text: They faced no serious opposition in the first century
choices.feedback: Not quite. Early sources describe threats, persecution, and violence.

choices.text: They kept their resurrection belief private and avoided public risk
choices.feedback: Not quite. The point is that they publicly proclaimed the message despite risk.

questions.explanation: We do not know with equal certainty that every original disciple willingly died for his belief. But early sources do indicate that the disciples publicly proclaimed the gospel despite threats, opposition, imprisonment, and violence. This supports the claim that they sincerely believed their resurrection testimony.

questions.question: What is the earliest written record we have of appearances of the risen Jesus?

choices.text: 1 Corinthians 15:3-8
choices.feedback: Correct. Paul's summary in 1 Corinthians 15 is our earliest written record of resurrection appearances.

choices.text: The Gospel of John
choices.feedback: Not quite. John is important, but 1 Corinthians is earlier.

choices.text: Josephus' Antiquities
choices.feedback: Not quite. Josephus is not the earliest written record of the appearances.

choices.text: The Nicene Creed
choices.feedback: Not quite. The Nicene Creed is much later.

questions.explanation: Paul's statement in 1 Corinthians 15:3-8 is earlier than the written Gospels and preserves a very early tradition about Jesus' death, burial, resurrection, and appearances.

questions.question: Why do many scholars hold that the original disciples claimed and believed that Jesus rose from the dead?

choices.text: Multiple sources report their claims, and some disciples suffered for proclaiming them
choices.feedback: Correct. The argument combines multiple attestation with evidence of sincere conviction.

choices.text: Because no historical sources are needed for this conclusion
choices.feedback: Not quite. The conclusion is based on historical sources.

choices.text: Because every later tradition about every apostle is equally certain
choices.feedback: Not quite. The argument does not require treating every later tradition as equally reliable.

choices.text: Because Roman officials officially endorsed the resurrection message
choices.feedback: Not quite. The early Christian message often faced opposition, not official endorsement.

questions.explanation: Many scholars grant that the original disciples claimed and believed Jesus had risen because multiple early sources attest to their proclamation, and because their willingness to suffer supports the sincerity of their belief.

questions.question: What distinguishes the original disciples' willingness to suffer from later martyrs or extremists who die for beliefs received from others?

choices.text: The disciples suffered for what they claimed to have personally seen, not merely for a tradition handed down to them
choices.feedback: Correct. The key distinction is firsthand witness versus inherited belief.

choices.text: Anyone who dies for a belief proves that the belief is true
choices.feedback: Not quite. Sincerity does not automatically prove truth.

choices.text: The disciples were never afraid or confused
choices.feedback: Not quite. The New Testament portrays the disciples as fearful and confused at points.

choices.text: There is no meaningful difference between firsthand witnesses and people who inherit a belief centuries later
choices.feedback: Not quite. Firsthand claims are historically different from inherited beliefs.

questions.explanation: The argument is not that martyrdom proves a belief true. Rather, the original disciples were in a position to know whether they had experiences they took to be appearances of the risen Jesus. Their willingness to suffer supports sincerity and helps challenge the idea that they knowingly invented the claim.

questions.question: Nine ancient sources attesting the disciples' claim that they had seen the risen Jesus can be grouped into which three categories?

choices.text: New Testament writings, oral tradition, and written tradition
choices.feedback: Correct. These are the three categories used to group the sources.

choices.text: Roman, Greek, and Egyptian government records
choices.feedback: Not quite. That is not the three-part grouping used here.

choices.text: Poetry, legal codes, and ancient coins
choices.feedback: Not quite. Those are not the categories in this argument.

choices.text: Medieval tradition, modern scholarship, and archaeology
choices.feedback: Not quite. The grouping concerns ancient sources connected to early Christian testimony.

questions.explanation: Habermas and Licona present the nine ancient sources in three categories: New Testament writings, oral tradition, and written tradition. The point is that the disciples' claim is not based on a single isolated source.

questions.question: Which two Apostolic Fathers report that the apostles were dramatically impacted by the resurrection of Jesus?

choices.text: Polycarp and Clement of Rome
choices.feedback: Correct. Polycarp and Clement are the pair in view.

choices.text: Augustine and Thomas Aquinas
choices.feedback: Not quite. They are major Christian thinkers, but they are not Apostolic Fathers from the earliest period.

choices.text: Martin Luther and John Calvin
choices.feedback: Not quite. They are Reformation figures, not Apostolic Fathers.

choices.text: Origen and Jerome
choices.feedback: Not quite. They are important early Christian writers, but not the two intended here.

questions.explanation: Polycarp and Clement of Rome are cited as early Christian voices reporting that the apostles were dramatically impacted by the resurrection of Jesus.

questions.question: The disciples' sincere belief that they had seen the risen Jesus helps weaken which two opposing theories?

choices.text: Conspiracy and legend or embellishment
choices.feedback: Correct. Sincere belief challenges fraud, and early testimony challenges late legendary development.

choices.text: Swoon theory and hallucination theory
choices.feedback: Not quite. Other arguments address those theories more directly.

choices.text: Mythicism and philosophical naturalism
choices.feedback: Not quite. Those are different kinds of objections.

choices.text: Aliens and time travel
choices.feedback: Creative, but not the two theories in view here.

questions.explanation: If the disciples sincerely believed they had seen the risen Jesus, that weakens the conspiracy theory because they were not knowingly lying. The early testimony also weakens the idea that resurrection belief was merely a late legend or embellishment.

questions.question: Bonus: A major medical article discussing the physical effects of scourging and crucifixion appeared in which journal?

choices.text: The Journal of the American Medical Association, March 21, 1986
choices.feedback: Correct. The article appeared in JAMA in 1986.

choices.text: The New England Journal of Medicine
choices.feedback: Not quite. The article in view was published in JAMA.

choices.text: The Lancet
choices.feedback: Not quite. The journal in view is JAMA.

choices.text: Popular Mechanics: Weekend Crucifixion Edition
choices.feedback: Not quite. Entertaining title, wrong publication.

questions.explanation: This bonus question points to the 1986 JAMA article often cited in discussions of the medical effects of scourging and crucifixion. It is marked as a bonus because it fits better under medical evidence for Jesus' death than under the main lesson category of early testimony and the disciples' conviction.

questions.question: Why are the testimonies of Paul and James especially significant?

choices.text: They had previously rejected or opposed the message of Jesus
choices.feedback: Correct. Their prior skepticism or opposition makes their later belief historically significant.

choices.text: They had never heard of Jesus before becoming Christians
choices.feedback: Not quite. The point is not that they were ignorant of Jesus, but that they had not accepted the Christian claim.

choices.text: They were Roman officials assigned to investigate Christianity
choices.feedback: Not quite. Paul and James were not Roman investigators.

choices.text: They personally wrote all four Gospels
choices.feedback: Not quite. Their importance does not depend on having written the Gospels.

questions.explanation: Paul had persecuted Christians, and James, the brother of Jesus, appears not to have believed in Jesus during his ministry. Their later commitment is significant because both came to believe after what they regarded as encounters with the risen Jesus.

questions.question: Which non-Christian first-century author reports the martyrdom of James, the brother of Jesus?

choices.text: Josephus
choices.feedback: Correct. Josephus reports the death of James, the brother of Jesus.

choices.text: Tacitus
choices.feedback: Not quite. Tacitus is important for other references to early Christianity, but Josephus is the source in view here.

choices.text: Pliny the Younger
choices.feedback: Not quite. Pliny discusses Christians, but not this report about James.

choices.text: Suetonius
choices.feedback: Not quite. Josephus is the first-century author connected to James's martyrdom.

questions.explanation: Josephus, a first-century Jewish historian and non-Christian source, reports the death of James, identifying him as the brother of Jesus who was called Christ.

questions.question: Which two skeptics came to believe after they thought the risen Jesus had appeared to them?

choices.text: James, the brother of Jesus, and Paul, the persecutor of Christians
choices.feedback: Correct. James and Paul are the two skeptics in view.

choices.text: Peter and John
choices.feedback: Not quite. Peter and John were disciples, not the two skeptics highlighted here.

choices.text: Pilate and Herod
choices.feedback: Not quite. They are not presented as converted resurrection witnesses.

choices.text: Thomas and Matthew
choices.feedback: Not quite. Thomas had doubts, but the pair in this argument is James and Paul.

questions.explanation: Habermas and Licona emphasize James and Paul because they were not followers in the same way as the original disciples. James had not believed in Jesus during his ministry, and Paul had actively opposed the church.

questions.question: Which first-century authors report the conversion of Paul?

choices.text: Paul and Luke
choices.feedback: Correct. Paul refers to his own conversion, and Luke narrates it in Acts.

choices.text: Josephus and Tacitus
choices.feedback: Not quite. They are important non-Christian sources, but not the sources in view for Paul's conversion.

choices.text: Mark and John
choices.feedback: Not quite. Paul and Luke are the two first-century authors in view.

choices.text: Clement and Polycarp
choices.feedback: Not quite. They are later early Christian writers, not the first-century pair intended here.

questions.explanation: Paul refers to his own former persecution of the church and his later calling, while Luke gives narrative accounts of Paul's conversion in Acts.

questions.question: According to Habermas and Licona, how many ancient sources report Paul's willingness to suffer and die for the gospel?

choices.text: Seven
choices.feedback: Correct. The number given in this argument is seven.

choices.text: Three
choices.feedback: Not quite. The number used in the argument is higher.

choices.text: Five
choices.feedback: Not quite. Habermas and Licona give seven in this argument.

choices.text: Twelve
choices.feedback: Not quite. That overstates the specific number in view.

questions.explanation: Habermas and Licona argue that seven ancient sources report Paul's willingness to suffer and die for the gospel. The point is that Paul's conversion was not casual or convenient; it led to costly commitment.

questions.question: Where is the appearance of the risen Jesus to James first reported?

choices.text: 1 Corinthians 15
choices.feedback: Correct. Paul mentions an appearance to James in 1 Corinthians 15.

choices.text: Acts 15
choices.feedback: Not quite. Acts 15 shows James as a leader, but the appearance to James is reported in 1 Corinthians 15.

choices.text: Mark 16
choices.feedback: Not quite. The source in view is Paul's summary in 1 Corinthians 15.

choices.text: Revelation
choices.feedback: Not quite. Revelation is not the first report of the appearance to James.

questions.explanation: In 1 Corinthians 15, Paul lists appearances of the risen Jesus and includes an appearance to James. This is significant because James had not been a believer during Jesus' public ministry.

questions.question: Which two ancient sources indicate that James, the brother of Jesus, became a leader in the Jerusalem church?

choices.text: Paul and Luke
choices.feedback: Correct. Paul and Luke both indicate James's important leadership role.

choices.text: Tacitus and Pliny
choices.feedback: Not quite. They are not the two sources in view for James's leadership in Jerusalem.

choices.text: Augustine and Jerome
choices.feedback: Not quite. These are later Christian writers, not the pair intended here.

choices.text: Luther and Calvin
choices.feedback: Not quite. They are Reformation figures, not ancient sources.

questions.explanation: Paul's letters and Luke's account in Acts indicate that James became a major leader in the Jerusalem church. This is historically significant given James's earlier unbelief.

questions.question: James, the brother of Jesus, was martyred for the gospel according to which ancient authors?

choices.text: Hegesippus
choices.feedback: True, but incomplete. Hegesippus is one of the sources, but not the only one listed.

choices.text: Josephus
choices.feedback: True, but incomplete. Josephus is one of the sources, but not the only one listed.

choices.text: Clement of Alexandria
choices.feedback: True, but incomplete. Clement of Alexandria is one of the sources, but not the only one listed.

choices.text: A, B, and C
choices.feedback: Correct. All three are included in the list of sources for James's martyrdom.

questions.explanation: Hegesippus, Josephus, and Clement of Alexandria are all cited in connection with the martyrdom of James. Because the answer choice refers to A, B, and C, this question should not shuffle its answer order.

questions.question: True or false: Jesus had a brother named James who did not believe in him prior to the resurrection.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. James is presented as Jesus' brother and as someone who did not believe during Jesus' ministry.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Not quite. The evidence indicates that James was Jesus' brother and was not a believer before the resurrection.

questions.explanation: The New Testament identifies James as Jesus' brother, and John 7:5 says Jesus' brothers did not believe in him during his ministry. James later appears as a leader in the Jerusalem church, which makes his change especially significant.

questions.question: What is an important difference between early skeptics like Paul and James and many modern skeptics?

choices.text: Paul and James changed because of what they regarded as direct encounters, while modern skeptics usually evaluate later testimony and evidence
choices.feedback: Correct. Paul and James were not merely evaluating a distant tradition; they believed they had encountered the risen Jesus.

choices.text: Modern skeptics are always more careful than ancient people
choices.feedback: Not quite. That is too broad and dismissive of ancient witnesses.

choices.text: Ancient people never questioned religious claims
choices.feedback: Not quite. Paul and James themselves show that skepticism existed in the first century.

choices.text: There is no meaningful difference between a firsthand claim and a later evaluation of evidence
choices.feedback: Not quite. Firsthand claims and later historical evaluation are different kinds of evidence.

questions.explanation: Modern skeptics typically assess Christianity from later testimony, documents, and arguments. Paul and James are different because they moved from rejection or opposition to belief after what they regarded as direct appearances of the risen Jesus.

questions.question: Bonus: If someone suggests Jesus' body left the tomb because he merely revived after crucifixion, what is the best response?

choices.text: That explanation fails as an inference to the best explanation given the evidence we have
choices.feedback: Correct. The survival theory does not explain the evidence as well as the resurrection hypothesis.

choices.text: Any possible explanation is just as good as any other
choices.feedback: Not quite. Historical reasoning weighs which explanation best accounts for the evidence.

choices.text: The medical and historical evidence should simply be ignored
choices.feedback: Not quite. The evidence surrounding scourging, crucifixion, burial, and appearances matters.

choices.text: Jesus probably found an ancient energy drink in the tomb
choices.feedback: Creative, but not a serious historical explanation.

questions.explanation: A survival or swoon-style theory must account for the severity of Roman scourging and crucifixion, Jesus' burial, the empty tomb claim, the disciples' belief that he appeared risen and glorified, and the conversions of Paul and James. As an inference to the best explanation, it is generally much weaker than the resurrection hypothesis.

lessons.title: The Empty Tomb: Evidence and Objections

questions.question: How does the Jewish leaders' claim that the disciples stole Jesus' body support the empty tomb?

choices.text: No one would need to explain the missing body unless the tomb was empty
choices.feedback: Correct. The stolen-body claim attempts to explain why the tomb was empty.

choices.text: It proves the disciples actually stole the body
choices.feedback: Not quite. The point is not that the accusation was true, but that it implies the body was missing.

choices.text: It shows the Jewish leaders denied that the tomb was empty
choices.feedback: Not quite. The accusation actually assumes the body was no longer in the tomb.

choices.text: It has no relevance to the empty tomb
choices.feedback: Not quite. A hostile explanation for the missing body is historically relevant.

questions.explanation: The stolen-body explanation is important because it does not simply deny the empty tomb. It tries to explain it. If Jesus' body were still in the tomb, there would have been no need to claim that the disciples stole it.

questions.question: Which ancient sources report or preserve the claim that the disciples stole Jesus' body?

choices.text: Matthew 28:12-13
choices.feedback: True, but incomplete. Matthew records the stolen-body explanation, but it is not the only source listed.

choices.text: Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho 108
choices.feedback: True, but incomplete. Justin Martyr refers to this accusation, but he is not the only source listed.

choices.text: Tertullian, De Spectaculis 30
choices.feedback: True, but incomplete. Tertullian also refers to the charge, but the best answer includes all three.

choices.text: A, B, and C
choices.feedback: Correct. All three sources are relevant to the stolen-body accusation.

questions.explanation: Matthew reports the original accusation, and later Christian writers such as Justin Martyr and Tertullian preserve knowledge of the charge that the disciples stole Jesus' body. Since the correct option refers to A, B, and C, this question should not shuffle its answers.

questions.question: If Jesus' body had been exhumed soon after burial, would it likely have been recognizable enough to refute the resurrection claim?

choices.text: Yes. His body would likely have been recognizable, and opponents could have used it to challenge the resurrection claim
choices.feedback: Correct. Producing the body would have been a powerful way to answer the resurrection proclamation.

choices.text: No. A body became completely unrecognizable immediately after burial
choices.feedback: Not quite. That overstates the effects of decomposition in the relevant time frame.

choices.text: No. The body would have been irrelevant to a bodily resurrection claim
choices.feedback: Not quite. A bodily resurrection claim is directly challenged if the body can be produced.

choices.text: No. Nobody in Jerusalem would have cared whether the body was still there
choices.feedback: Not quite. The resurrection proclamation began in a context where the claim could be contested.

questions.explanation: If Jesus' corpse had been available, opponents of the resurrection message would have had a strong reason to produce it. The recognizability of the body, especially with crucifixion wounds, matters because it would have provided a direct challenge to the claim that Jesus had been raised.

questions.question: The women are reported as witnesses to the empty tomb in all four Gospels. How many Gospels also report men as witnesses to the empty tomb?

choices.text: Two
choices.feedback: Correct. Two Gospels report male witnesses connected to the empty tomb.

choices.text: One
choices.feedback: Not quite. The number is higher than one.

choices.text: Three
choices.feedback: Not quite. The intended answer is two.

choices.text: Four
choices.feedback: Not quite. All four report women, but not all four report men as witnesses to the empty tomb.

questions.explanation: All four Gospels report women as witnesses to the empty tomb. The presence of women in all four accounts is significant for the principle of embarrassment, while the male-witness detail is not reported in all four.

questions.question: The empty tomb is best described as:

choices.text: The one fact that by itself proves the resurrection
choices.feedback: Not quite. The empty tomb is important, but the case for the resurrection is cumulative.

choices.text: A historical bedrock fact on the same level as Jesus' death and the disciples' experiences
choices.feedback: Not quite. The empty tomb is significant, but it is usually treated differently from the core minimal facts.

choices.text: A second-order fact that supports the resurrection hypothesis
choices.feedback: Correct. The empty tomb supports the resurrection hypothesis without carrying the whole case by itself.

choices.text: A late legend with no historical value
choices.feedback: Not quite. The empty tomb is argued from several historical considerations, including enemy attestation and the women witnesses.

questions.explanation: The empty tomb is best treated as an important supporting fact in the overall case for the resurrection. It does not by itself carry the whole argument, but it strengthens the resurrection hypothesis when considered alongside Jesus' death, the disciples' experiences, early proclamation, and the conversions of Paul and James.

questions.question: True or false: The bodily resurrection could only have been proclaimed in Jerusalem if the tomb was in fact empty.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. A public bodily resurrection claim would have been vulnerable if the body were still in the tomb.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Not quite. A bodily resurrection claim is difficult to sustain publicly if the body can be produced.

questions.explanation: A bodily resurrection proclamation in Jerusalem would have faced an obvious challenge if Jesus' body remained in the tomb. The empty tomb is therefore an important part of the historical case.

questions.question: How is the principle of embarrassment used to support the empty tomb?

choices.text: A fictional account would be less likely to make women the first discoverers of the empty tomb in that cultural setting
choices.feedback: Correct. The women witnesses are often used as an example of the principle of embarrassment.

choices.text: The story makes the religious leaders look especially heroic
choices.feedback: Not quite. The principle concerns details that would be awkward or less convenient for the source.

choices.text: The story avoids naming anyone connected to the empty tomb
choices.feedback: Not quite. The issue here is the reported role of women as witnesses.

choices.text: The accounts use the most socially powerful witnesses possible
choices.feedback: Not quite. The argument is that women were not the witnesses one would invent if trying to make the story maximally persuasive in that setting.

questions.explanation: In the cultural context of the time, women were not typically viewed as the strongest public witnesses. The fact that all four Gospels report women as the first witnesses to the empty tomb is therefore argued to be less likely as an invention.

questions.question: How is the principle of enemy attestation used to support the empty tomb?

choices.text: The Jewish leadership indirectly attested to the empty tomb by accusing the disciples of stealing the body
choices.feedback: Correct. A hostile explanation for the missing body implies that the body was missing.

choices.text: The Jewish leadership proved the tomb was occupied by producing Jesus' body
choices.feedback: Not quite. Producing the body would have challenged the empty tomb, but that is not what the stolen-body claim does.

choices.text: Only friendly Christian sources matter for this argument
choices.feedback: Not quite. Enemy attestation is significant precisely because it comes from hostile or unsympathetic sources.

choices.text: Enemy attestation means there were no hostile responses to the resurrection claim
choices.feedback: Not quite. The stolen-body claim is itself a hostile response.

questions.explanation: Enemy attestation is used here because the stolen-body accusation comes from opponents of the Christian movement. Their accusation attempts to explain why the tomb was empty, thereby indirectly supporting the empty tomb itself.

questions.question: What is the strongest reason to think Jesus' corpse would still have been identifiable even as late as fifty days after burial?

choices.text: His body would likely be identifiable by crucifixion wounds and the effects of the manner of death
choices.feedback: Correct. The distinctive wounds and manner of death would matter for identification.

choices.text: Roman victims were buried with official name tags
choices.feedback: Not quite. The argument does not depend on an official name tag.

choices.text: A corpse would show no decomposition after fifty days
choices.feedback: Not quite. The point is not that decomposition would be absent, but that identifying features could remain.

choices.text: Only the burial cloths would have been needed to identify him
choices.feedback: Not quite. Burial context may matter, but the argument focuses on identifiable wounds and the manner of death.

questions.explanation: The argument is that Jesus' body would likely have had distinctive signs of scourging and crucifixion. Those marks would be relevant to identification, even if the body had begun to decompose.

questions.question: True or false: If something cannot be made 100% certain, we should not accept it as true.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Not quite. Historical reasoning rarely gives mathematical certainty, but it can still justify belief.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Correct. We often accept conclusions on strong evidence without requiring 100% certainty.

questions.explanation: Historical claims are usually evaluated by evidence, probability, explanatory power, and comparison with rival explanations. Requiring 100% certainty would rule out many things we reasonably accept as true.
